content.text: The Microsoft Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) publishes a standard list of resource type abbreviations designed for use as the first segment of a resource name. These are short (2–5 character) lowercase strings that uniquely identify the resource type at a glance. Adopting the CAF abbreviations means your names will be consistent with Microsoft documentation, third-party tooling expectations, and the naming pattern used in Azure landing zone accelerators.

attrs.alt: CAF recommended resource abbreviations by category

content.text: Adopting the CAF abbreviations whole-cloth is better than inventing custom ones, because new team members already know the standard and tools like Bicep landing zone modules use the same prefixes.
Include target resource type after pe-
NO hyphens; 3-24 lowercase alphanum; globally unique
Generate a markdown naming reference that your team can keep in the repository alongside the IaC code. This serves as an onboarding reference and a policy attachment for new team members.

# Generate a naming reference cheat-sheet from your chosen abbreviations
# Useful for onboarding and as a policy attachment

cat > naming-cheatsheet.md << 'EOF'
# Azure Resource Naming Cheat-Sheet

## Pattern: {abbr}-{workload}-{env}-{region}[-{seq}]

| Resource Type | Abbreviation | Example Name | Notes |
|----|----|----|---|
| Virtual Machine | vm | vm-payments-prod-eastus-001 | Windows: max 15 chars |
| VM Scale Set | vmss | vmss-payments-prod-eastus | |
| AKS Cluster | aks | aks-payments-prod-eastus | |
| Function App | func | func-payments-prod-eastus | Globally unique |
| Virtual Network | vnet | vnet-payments-prod-eastus | |
| Subnet | snet | snet-payments-app-prod-eastus | Include layer |
| NSG | nsg | nsg-payments-app-prod-eastus | |
| Public IP | pip | pip-payments-prod-eastus | |
| Load Balancer | lb | lb-payments-prod-eastus | |
| App Gateway | agw | agw-payments-prod-eastus | |
| Storage Account | st | st0paymentsprodeus | No hyphens; max 24 chars |
| Key Vault | kv | kv-payments-prod-eus | Globally unique; max 24 |
| ACR | acr | acrpaymentsprodeus | No hyphens; globally unique |
| SQL Server | sql | sql-payments-prod-eastus | |
| SQL Database | sqldb | sqldb-payments-prod-eastus | |
| Cosmos DB | cosmos | cosmos-payments-prod-eastus | |
| App Service | app | app-payments-prod-eastus | Globally unique |
| App Service Plan | plan | plan-payments-prod-eastus | |
| API Management | apim | apim-payments-prod-eastus | Globally unique |
| Container Instance | aci | aci-payments-prod-eastus | |
| Container Apps | ca | ca-payments-prod-eastus | |
| Log Analytics Workspace | log | log-payments-prod-eastus | |
| App Insights | appi | appi-payments-prod-eastus | |
| Resource Group | rg | rg-payments-prod-eastus | |
EOF
echo 'Created naming-cheatsheet.md'

Store all approved abbreviations in a dedicated Bicep module (abbr.bicep) that outputs a single object. All other modules in the repository import this module and use the output object to build names. This guarantees that every resource type uses the correct abbreviation regardless of which engineer authors the template.

In Terraform, define the abbreviations as a locals map in a shared locals.tf file. All resource definitions in the workspace reference local.abbr.<type> to form the type prefix. This ensures any future abbreviation change only requires editing one place.

# Terraform: abbreviations as a local map
# locals.tf — single source of truth for all prefix abbreviations

locals {
  abbr = {
    # Compute
    virtual_machine          = "vm"
    vmss                     = "vmss"
    aks_cluster              = "aks"
    function_app             = "func"
    app_service              = "app"
    app_service_plan         = "plan"
    container_instance       = "aci"
    container_app            = "ca"

    # Networking
    virtual_network          = "vnet"
    subnet                   = "snet"
    nsg                      = "nsg"
    public_ip                = "pip"
    load_balancer            = "lb"
    application_gateway      = "agw"
    azure_firewall           = "afw"
    private_endpoint         = "pe"
    route_table              = "rt"
    bastion                  = "bas"

    # Storage
    storage_account          = "st"      # no separator in name
    managed_disk             = "disk"
    recovery_vault           = "rsv"

    # Databases
    sql_server               = "sql"
    sql_database             = "sqldb"
    cosmos_db                = "cosmos"
    redis_cache              = "redis"
    postgres_server          = "psql"

    # Security
    key_vault                = "kv"
    user_managed_identity    = "id"
    container_registry       = "acr"     # no separator in name

    # Integration
    api_management           = "apim"
    logic_app                = "logic"
    service_bus              = "sbns"
    event_hub_namespace      = "evhns"

    # Monitoring
    log_analytics            = "log"
    app_insights             = "appi"
    automation_account       = "aa"
    resource_group           = "rg"
  }
}

# Usage in resource definitions:
# resource "azurerm_key_vault" "main" {
#   name = "${local.abbr.key_vault}-${var.workload}-${var.environment}-${var.region_abbr}"
# }
#
# resource "azurerm_storage_account" "main" {
#   # Storage: no hyphens, prefix only, max 24 chars
#   name = "${local.abbr.storage_account}${var.workload}${var.environment}${var.region_abbr}"
# }
Enforcing Abbreviation Prefixes with Azure Policy

Azure Policy's match condition supports prefix wildcards (vm-*, vnet-*, kv-*) that can deny deployments when the resource name does not begin with the correct abbreviation. Assign these policies at the management group level so they cover all subscriptions. Use Deny for most types and Audit for storage accounts and ACR where the constraint is harder to express as a single wildcard pattern.
Auditing Prefix Compliance with Resource Graph

Resource Graph's !startswith operator and join against a datatable of type-to-prefix rules lets you build a consolidated compliance report across all resource types in one query. Run this weekly as part of a governance review or pipe the output to a storage account for historical trending.

// Resource Graph: find resources that do not use the correct CAF abbreviation prefix

// 1. VMs not starting with 'vm-'
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines'
| where name !startswith 'vm-'
| project name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId
| order by subscriptionId asc, name asc

// 2. VNets not starting with 'vnet-'
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks'
| where name !startswith 'vnet-'
| project name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId

// 3. NSGs not starting with 'nsg-'
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.network/networksecuritygroups'
| where name !startswith 'nsg-'
| project name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId

// 4. Consolidated prefix compliance report across multiple types
let typeRules = datatable(resourceType:string, expectedPrefix:string) [
    'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines',        'vm-',
    'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks',         'vnet-',
    'microsoft.network/networksecuritygroups',   'nsg-',
    'microsoft.keyvault/vaults',                 'kv-',
    'microsoft.compute/disks',                   'disk-',
    'microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters','aks-',
    'microsoft.web/serverfarms',                 'plan-',
    'microsoft.web/sites',                       'app-',
];
Resources
| where type in (typeRules | project resourceType)
| join kind=inner (typeRules) on $left.type == $right.resourceType
| where not(name startswith expectedPrefix)
| project name, type, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, expectedPrefix
| order by type asc, subscriptionId asc

Add a pre-deployment validation step in your CI/CD pipeline that checks each resource name in the Bicep parameters file against the expected prefix for its type. This catches mistakes before the deployment reaches ARM and before any Deny policy fires. A Bash associative array maps resource types to their required prefix; the validate_name function returns a non-zero exit code on mismatch, which causes the pipeline stage to fail.

# Shell script: validate a name against the expected prefix for its resource type
# Use in CI/CD pipelines before az resource create runs

declare -A EXPECTED_PREFIX
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines']='vm-'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachineScaleSets']='vmss-'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks']='vnet-'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.Network/networkSecurityGroups']='nsg-'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.Network/publicIPAddresses']='pip-'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.Network/loadBalancers']='lb-'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.Network/applicationGateways']='agw-'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults']='kv-'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts']='st'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.ContainerRegistry/registries']='acr'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.ContainerService/managedClusters']='aks-'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.Web/sites']='app-'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.Web/serverFarms']='plan-'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces']='log-'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.Insights/components']='appi-'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.Sql/servers']='sql-'
EXPECTED_PREFIX['Microsoft.Resources/resourceGroups']='rg-'

validate_name() {
  local RESOURCE_TYPE=$1
  local NAME=$2
  local PREFIX=${EXPECTED_PREFIX[$RESOURCE_TYPE]}

  if [[ -z "$PREFIX" ]]; then
    echo "WARNING: No prefix rule for $RESOURCE_TYPE — skipping prefix check"
    return 0
  fi

  if [[ "$NAME" == "$PREFIX"* ]]; then
    echo "PASS: '$NAME' uses correct prefix '$PREFIX'"
    return 0
  else
    echo "FAIL: '$NAME' should start with '$PREFIX' for type $RESOURCE_TYPE"
    return 1
  fi
}

# Example usage:
validate_name 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines' 'vm-payments-prod-eastus-001'  # PASS
validate_name 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines' 'payments-vm-prod-001'          # FAIL
validate_name 'Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults' 'kv-payments-prod-eus'                  # PASS
validate_name 'Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts' 'st0paymentsprodeus'            # PASS
validate_name 'Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts' 'sa-payments-prod-eus'          # FAIL (st not sa)

Azure region names (eastus, westeurope, australiaeast) are too long for resource names, especially when combined with the type prefix, workload, and environment. Define a short abbreviation for every region your organization uses and encode the map in your naming module. CAF does not mandate specific region abbreviations, so choose consistently short forms (3–4 characters) and document them alongside the type abbreviations.

# Standard Azure region abbreviations for use in resource names
# These are organization conventions — CAF does not mandate a specific list.
# Choose short forms and document them in your naming module.

declare -A REGION_ABBR

# Americas
REGION_ABBR['eastus']='eus'
REGION_ABBR['eastus2']='eus2'
REGION_ABBR['westus']='wus'
REGION_ABBR['westus2']='wus2'
REGION_ABBR['westus3']='wus3'
REGION_ABBR['centralus']='cus'
REGION_ABBR['northcentralus']='ncus'
REGION_ABBR['southcentralus']='scus'
REGION_ABBR['canadacentral']='cnc'
REGION_ABBR['brazilsouth']='brs'

# Europe
REGION_ABBR['northeurope']='neu'
REGION_ABBR['westeurope']='weu'
REGION_ABBR['uksouth']='uks'
REGION_ABBR['ukwest']='ukw'
REGION_ABBR['francecentral']='frc'
REGION_ABBR['germanywestcentral']='gwc'
REGION_ABBR['swedencentral']='sec'
REGION_ABBR['switzerlandnorth']='shn'

# Asia Pacific
REGION_ABBR['eastasia']='ea'
REGION_ABBR['southeastasia']='sea'
REGION_ABBR['australiaeast']='aue'
REGION_ABBR['australiasoutheast']='ause'
REGION_ABBR['japaneast']='jpe'
REGION_ABBR['koreacentral']='krc'
REGION_ABBR['centralindia']='inc'

# Example: build a name using the abbreviation map
REGION='eastus'
ABBR=${REGION_ABBR[$REGION]:-$REGION}  # fallback to full name if not in map
NAME="vm-payments-prod-${ABBR}-001"
echo "$NAME"   # vm-payments-prod-eus-001
Using 'sa' instead of 'st' for storage accounts
CAF standard is 'st'; 'sa' is inconsistent with community tooling
Using 'vm' without the type separator for non-storage types
Unreadable; does not allow word separation in search filters
Using full region name in the name
Long region names push Windows VM names (max 15 chars) over the limit
CAF uses 'app' for App Service; 'webapp' is non-standard
Inconsistency breaks KQL prefix filters and policy match rules
Abbreviating the workload so aggressively the name is ambiguous
Abbreviate only what necessary to meet length limits; legibility first

The CAF abbreviation list provides a short (2–5 character) lowercase prefix for every common Azure resource type. Use these as the first segment of every resource name so the type is immediately visible in the portal, CLI output, and billing reports. Store the abbreviations in a single Bicep module (abbr.bicep) or Terraform locals map so all templates derive prefixes from one authoritative source. Note that storage accounts, ACR, and some other globally-unique types prohibit hyphens — the abbreviation is used as a plain prefix with no separator before the workload segment.
